text: Iron has emerged as a promising replacement metal for expensive transition metals in photoactive compounds. However, there remains a considerable challenge in designing iron(III) complexes with emissive properties and reactivity, owing to the high rate of deactivation of the charge-transfer (CT) state. Emissive iron(III) compounds are scarce and examples of dual emission have never been reported so far. Herein, we present a novel cyclometalated Fe(III) complex, [Fe(ImP)₂][PF₆], achieved via judicious design of a tridentate ligand bearing four N-heterocyclic carbenes (NHCs) with two cyclometalating aryl rings at the periphery, displaying “Janus-type” dual emission behavior through both ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) and metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) excited states. Moreover, the lifetime of the MLCT dominated state of this complex is as long as 4.6 ns endows with excellent reductive and oxidative ability, facilitating the bimolecular electron transfer process. Our work sheds new light on the rational design of iron-based luminophores, offering immense possibilities in the field of sustainable photoredox catalysis.
explanation: Polished, domain-appropriate scientific prose with plausible details. A few awkward phrasings and an absolutist claim about novelty without citations mildly suggest AI generation.
